children.name: Trichoglossus
children.description: Vividly coloured, medium-sized parrots with a long, narrow bill and a specialised brush-tipped tongue adapted for feeding on nectar and pollen. Lorikeets are among the most brilliantly plumaged of all parrots, combining blues, greens, yellows, and reds in dazzling patterns. They are highly social and gather in noisy, boisterous flocks.

children.name: Trichoglossus haematodus
children.description: One of the most colourful and widely recognised parrots, found from Indonesia and the Philippines through New Guinea to eastern Australia. Its plumage is a spectacular combination of blue head, green wings, yellow collar, and red breast. It is a common bird in Australian cities, where it visits garden feeders in noisy flocks.

children.name: Melopsittacus
children.description: A monotypic genus of small, social parrot that has become one of the most popular pet birds in the world. In the wild, Budgerigars are nomadic, following rainfall and food availability across the vast Australian interior. Their name comes from an Australian Aboriginal word meaning 'good food'.

children.name: Melopsittacus undulatus
children.description: A small, green-and-yellow parrot endemic to Australia's arid interior, where it occurs in huge nomadic flocks. It has a distinctive undulating flight pattern and a chattering, musical call. In captivity it has been bred into a vast array of colours and is the third most popular pet animal after dogs and cats.

children.name: Agapornis
children.description: Small, stocky, brightly-coloured parrots native to Africa and Madagascar, named for their strong pair bonds and affectionate behaviour — they sit close together and preen each other constantly. Lovebirds sleep side by side and form some of the most enduring pair bonds in the bird world.

children.name: Agapornis roseicollis
children.description: A small, vividly coloured parrot native to the arid regions of Namibia, South Africa, and Angola. It has a bright green body, a blue rump, and a distinctive rosy-pink face and throat. It is one of the most popular pet parrots worldwide, known for its affectionate nature and high intelligence.

children.name: Psittacula
children.description: Slender, long-tailed parakeets with a distinctive ring or collar around the neck, found across Africa and Asia. They are highly adaptable birds, often thriving in urban environments well beyond their natural range. Several species have established feral populations in cities across Europe and North America.

children.name: Psittacula eupatria
children.description: One of the largest parakeets, found from Sri Lanka and India through Southeast Asia to Indochina. It has a striking green body, a massive red bill, and a distinctive pink-and-black collar. Named after Alexander the Great, who is said to have transported them to Europe in the 4th century BC.

children.name: Psittacula krameri
children.description: The most widely distributed parrot species, naturally ranging across Africa and Asia, with established feral populations in Europe, North America, and Japan. It has a bright green body, a red bill, and a distinctive black-and-pink collar. In London and other European cities, its loud squawks have become a familiar urban sound.

children.name: Eclectus
children.description: A monotypic genus famous for extreme sexual dimorphism — the male is bright green with a coral-red bill, while the female is vivid red and purple with a black bill. For nearly a century, the two sexes were thought to be entirely different species. They are frugivorous parrots of lowland rainforest.

children.name: Eclectus roratus
children.description: A large, stocky parrot found from the Moluccas and New Guinea to the Solomon Islands and northern Australia. The male is a brilliant emerald green with red and blue wing patches; the female is a striking crimson and purple. They feed primarily on fruit, with a specialised digestive system that allows them to process a wide variety of tropical fruits.

children.name: Psephotus
children.description: Small, slender parrots with long tails and a semi-terrestrial foraging habit, endemic to Australia. They are known for their gentle, musical calls and beautiful plumage, combining blues, greens, yellows, and reds. The Red-rumped Parrot is one of the most commonly kept Australian parrots in aviculture.

children.name: Psephotus haematonotus
children.description: A common and beautiful parrot of south-eastern Australia, found in open woodlands, grasslands, and urban parks. The male has a bright green body with a yellow belly and a distinctive red patch on the rump; the female is duller olive-green. It feeds on grass seeds and forages on the ground in small flocks.
